title: My personal journey to using GitHub Copilot so far…
author: David Lee
text:
My personal journey to using GitHub Copilot so far…
This story is handwritten and no part of it has been written by AI (except the image, which is generated by Copilot). Nothing against AI here. It’s how I feel I can be authentic.
For the last couple of months, I have been using GitHub Copilot professionally. I am fortunate enough to be able to work in an organization where I can work with different organizations and projects and for the latest project, GitHub Copilot is an option. I have to admit at first, I was slow to adopt, thinking that it will be painful to get the prompts right and I have to spend cycles to iterate on it at a critical point of the project. However, pass that point, I found more time and I did have time to experiment.
Starting small
I work with many awesome individuals and every week; I have the opportunity to learn from their experiences. There are suggestions such as to include GitHub instructions and there’s even a site dedicated to sharing instructions. Many spoke about MCP because it is so awesome and allows you to do so much more in an automated fashion. Many folks have also commented on starting with Claude Sonnet as a starting point.
It’s a lot. My thought (and suggestion here) is to YANGI it. Make the experience as frictionless as possible for yourself first. So, I started with just Claude Sonnet 3.7 which is just an easy dropdown selection in VS Code (and later Claude Sonnet 4). This way, I can truly understand what I need and when I need more capabilities. It’s about the use cases.
Small changes, please!
For context, I want to state that the two languages I am using are .NET and Python. I am truly impressed by how Claude Sonnet is able to take my prompt and work out a solution without additional GitHub instructions.
I would like to talk about the Planner approach where we can also ask Copilot to produce a doc on how it plans to implement and continue with the change. This usually requires a Github instruction for better performance. I do not think this approach is unreasonable, but I do think the doc is usually pretty extensive and require careful review. The resulting code changes are pretty big and can cover several areas. Of course, this is all dependent on the requirements and large changes can be expected.
Personally, I do not like big code changes to review. The way I prefer to work is to provide enough instructions that I feel would allow Copilot to get started and then I can continue iterating with small changes and guide the update process. This way, I am aware of every line of code written and I can guide my thought process of any logical issues or edge cases that may not be apparent. It also allows me the opportunity to run the code if I need to test out some assumptions at that point of the state of the code.
No, I will write myself
Copilot can definitely help us big or small changes. But not everything needs to be written by copilot. There are many simple straight-forward changes that can be done manually like Find and Replace. Rename can be done by refactor in VS Code. There are examples of small blocks of code that can be written/or refactored manually since we already have the context. I found the time and effort in comparison between writing the prompt versus manual is negligible in a lot of cases. On a personal level, it’s also about saving the environment for the next generation and using only what I need!
Let’s YAGNI it
Copilot makes it easy for us to add bells and whistles. For a python console application I wrote, a co-worker suggested adding a progress bar. On the surface, it seemed like a quick win and easy to do. I was able to quickly prompt Copilot to add it within minutes and it works. Then I thought about the edge cases. What if the process failed in the middle. There needs to be some way to also propagate the error back to the progress bar. Obviously, there are many ways to solve the issue. However, the maintainer (who is not me) of the code a) does not require a progress bar for his use-case, and b) may not have the expertise to troubleshoot since his role is mainly on the QA side. The code at hand should be easily understood without the additional complexity.
Even though Copilot makes it easy for us to enhance it new capabilities, we as engineers have to keep our users in mind and focus on the problem we are solving.
Let’s automate it
I really hate to repeat work that I need to do over and over again manually. I would try to find time to automate process by writing automation scripts (think Python or PowerShell scripts). Trying to get the process right could take hours.
Now, it is a breeze to use GitHub Copilot to write those same scripts that could take hours. I guess this is not a surprise. However, there is a point I am making here. Instead of writing the automation scripts, how about having GitHub Copilot execute the work instead through a prompt like “Create a new .NET API project that allows me to do XYZ”. Maybe we can sprinkle on some MCP magic to let it review the latest .NET docs so it can potentially grab the latest coding patterns. Alternatively, we can also ask GitHub Copilot to write automation scripts to generate .NET Templates that we can maintain. If you don’t know, .NET Templates are, they allow you to create .NET project by you defining your custom files following your conventions. Obviously, there are tradeoffs to consider, but my personal preference is to keep automation as deterministic as possible, and I prefer to use automation scripts for this reason.
Conclusion
I am convinced that GitHub Copilot with Claude Sonnet makes me/us more productive with intentional practices that I mentioned in this story. At the end of the day, we are the author of the code even if the code is generated by AI. We are the one who commits the code into the repository and when there is an issue, we are ultimately responsible.
I would like to leave with you this thought: Let’s avoid the phrase AI wrote the code and trend towards I wrote the code. I personally think this mindset can help us leverage AI best. Thank you for reading!
